article_title: Sodium-Ion Batteries Reach the US Grid as Peak Energy and RWE Launch First Pilot on the MISO Network

content_preview.title: Sodium-Ion Batteries Reach the US Grid as Peak Energy and RWE Launch First Pilot on the MISO Network
content_preview.summary: Peak Energy and RWE Americas have deployed the first sodium-ion battery system on the MISO grid in Wisconsin, marking a milestone for the cheaper, cobalt-free chemistry in the US.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Sodium-ion battery manufacturer Peak Energy and global energy company RWE Americas have begun piloting a sodium-ion battery storage system on the Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO) grid in eastern Wisconsin, marking the first deployment of the chemistry on one of North America's largest interconnected power networks. The project uses Peak Energy's GS1.1 system, which requires no active cooling and operates across a wide temperature range, according to [Electrek](https:/...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article covering the first US MISO grid deployment of sodium-ion batteries with good technical and economic detail.
editor_notes.source_verification: Electrek confirms Peak Energy/RWE MISO deployment, GS1.1 system, $70/kWh cost reduction, Aurora Energy $27B analysis, Mossburg quotes, RWE 13GW capacity. ScienceDaily confirms University of Surrey research, Commandeur 'completely unexpected' quote, 400 cycles, charge doubling. BatteriesNews confirmed reachable by pipeline.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All verifiable claims confirmed against sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and balanced, with appropriate caveats about unknowns.
editor_notes.originality: Existing sodium-ion article from March 2 focuses on CATL/BYD commercial scale in China. This new article covers a distinct angle: first US MISO grid deployment by Peak Energy/RWE, plus University of Surrey cathode research. Sufficiently different to approve.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong energy tech article with verified sources and distinct angle from existing coverage. Approved.
